sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: Hypericum perforatum inhibits serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine reuptake and, when co-administered with SSRIs such as sertraline, produces additive serotonergic effect. Multiple case reports describe serotonin syndrome (hyperthermia, clonus, agitation) after concurrent use; MSK explicitly lists SSRIs among contraindicated classes.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: Concurrent paroxetine plus St. John's Wort has been associated with lethargy, incoherence, and serotonin-syndrome features in published case reports. MSK advises against combining Hypericum with any serotonergic antidepressant.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: SNRIs share the serotonin-reuptake-inhibition target of SSRIs, so adding St. John's Wort compounds the serotonergic load. Case reports describe agitation and autonomic instability when venlafaxine is combined with Hypericum.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: MAO inhibitors plus Hypericum risk hypertensive crisis and severe serotonin syndrome; MSK lists MAOIs as a hard contraindication. A 14-day washout is recommended after MAOI discontinuation before introducing serotonergic herbs.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: Triptans are 5-HT1B/1D agonists and, combined with St. John's Wort's reuptake inhibition, can precipitate serotonin syndrome, especially in migraine patients using frequent triptan doses.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: Tramadol has intrinsic serotonin-reuptake-inhibition activity; combining with Hypericum has produced documented serotonin-syndrome presentations. Risk is highest at >300 mg/day tramadol.

5-HTP is the immediate precursor of serotonin and bypasses tryptophan hydroxylase regulation. Co-administration with SSRIs such as fluoxetine raises cerebral 5-HT to levels reported to cause serotonin syndrome; MSK lists SSRIs as a contraindication.

MAOIs block serotonin catabolism; pairing with the serotonin precursor 5-HTP can cause rapid serotonin accumulation and potentially fatal hyperthermia. The combination is contraindicated in MSK guidance.

SAMe has intrinsic antidepressant activity and may enhance monoaminergic signaling. Case reports describe serotonin-syndrome features when combined with SSRIs; MSK cautions against pairing SAMe with 5-HTP, SJW, or SSRIs.

L-tryptophan loads the serotonin-synthesis pathway upstream of 5-HTP. In the presence of MAOIs, catabolism is blocked and serotonin accumulates, producing hyperreflexia, confusion, and hyperthermia consistent with serotonin syndrome.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: Duloxetine inhibits serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake; Hypericum contributes additional reuptake inhibition and CYP-mediated changes in duloxetine metabolism. MSK advises avoiding concurrent use.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: Hyperforin is a potent PXR ligand and induces CYP3A4, accelerating oestrogen and progestin clearance. Randomised PK studies and reported breakthrough bleeding / unplanned pregnancies establish a clinically significant contraceptive failure risk.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: CYP3A4 induction by SJW reduces cyclosporine AUC by up to 50%, with published reports of acute transplant rejection. FDA and EMA have issued safety communications; combination is contraindicated in transplant recipients.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: A landmark NIH PK study showed SJW reduced indinavir AUC by 57% via CYP3A4 induction, risking virologic failure in HIV patients. Antiretroviral co-administration with SJW is contraindicated.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: SJW induces CYP2C9 (the main metaboliser of S-warfarin) and CYP3A4, lowering INR and causing subtherapeutic anticoagulation. Published case series show loss of INR control within 1–2 weeks of starting SJW.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: Simvastatin is a CYP3A4 substrate; SJW induction reduces simvastatin and its active metabolite plasma exposure, blunting LDL-lowering efficacy. Pravastatin (non-CYP3A4) is a preferred alternative.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: Tacrolimus trough levels depend heavily on intestinal P-glycoprotein activity, which hyperforin induces. Although CYP3A4 induction contributes, P-gp induction is the dominant mechanism for the ~50% trough-level drop reported in transplant recipients, with documented rejection episodes.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: Imatinib AUC drops ~30–40% with SJW co-administration via CYP3A4 induction, potentially compromising CML control. Oncology guidelines explicitly prohibit SJW with tyrosine-kinase inhibitors.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: SJW reduces exposure of SN-38 (the active metabolite of irinotecan) by ~40% through CYP3A4 induction and UGT1A1 modulation, with persistence up to 3 weeks after SJW discontinuation — risking chemotherapy failure.

Furanocoumarins in grapefruit juice irreversibly inhibit intestinal CYP3A4, raising felodipine AUC by 2–3 fold and producing hypotension and reflex tachycardia in controlled studies. Effect persists up to 72 h after ingestion.
Intestinal CYP3A4 inhibition by grapefruit raises simvastatin AUC up to 15-fold, dramatically increasing rhabdomyolysis risk. Statin labeling explicitly cautions against grapefruit intake.
Grapefruit inhibition of enterocyte CYP3A4 can elevate tacrolimus trough levels into nephrotoxic range in transplant patients; hidden grapefruit in beverages has been implicated in published cases.

Goldenseal produces the largest in-vivo CYP3A4 inhibition of any commonly used botanical (~40% midazolam AUC increase in controlled PK studies), intensifying sedative effect and respiratory depression risk.
Goldenseal inhibits CYP2D6 in vivo, increasing metoprolol Cmax and AUC and raising risk of bradycardia and hypotension. MSK flags goldenseal as a broad CYP inhibitor.
Kavalactones modulate CYP1A2, CYP2C9, CYP2C19 and CYP3A4 and also have intrinsic GABAergic effect. Case reports describe semi-coma when kava was added to alprazolam therapy.

A randomised placebo-controlled crossover trial showed 2 weeks of American ginseng reduced INR and warfarin AUC in healthy volunteers. The effect is pharmacodynamic rather than CYP-mediated, likely reflecting induced synthesis of vitamin-K-dependent clotting factors by ginsenosides.
Silymarin weakly inhibits CYP3A4 and UGT1A1, potentially reducing indinavir exposure. Evidence is mixed but MSK lists HIV protease inhibitors among medications to discuss before use.
Curcumin inhibits CYP3A4 and CYP2D6, potentially lowering endoxifen (tamoxifen's active metabolite) formation. MSK explicitly warns about tamoxifen interactions and reduced chemotherapy efficacy.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — Epimedium (Horny Goat Weed)

notes: Icariin, the main epimedium flavonoid, is a phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor with vasodilator activity. Added to a calcium-channel blocker this produces additive hypotension; MSK lists antihypertensives and PDE5-dependent drugs as interaction concerns.

Glycyrrhizin produces apparent-mineralocorticoid-excess via 11β-HSD2 inhibition, directly opposing the aldosterone-antagonist action of spironolactone. Case reports describe blunted diuresis and unexpected hypokalaemia when licorice is co-used with K-sparing diuretics.

Ginkgolides inhibit platelet-activating factor, which combined with warfarin-driven anticoagulation has produced subdural haematoma, intracerebral bleed, and postoperative haemorrhage in published case reports. MSK advises discontinuation 1–2 weeks pre-surgery.
Additive antiplatelet effect: ginkgolide B blocks PAF-mediated aggregation while aspirin inhibits COX-1. Published cases link concurrent use to spontaneous hyphema and intracranial bleeding.

Allicin-derived compounds inhibit platelet aggregation and enhance fibrinolysis, raising INR when combined with warfarin. Post-operative haemorrhage has been reported; MSK recommends stopping garlic supplements 1 week before surgery.
Concomitant garlic and clopidogrel produce additive antiplatelet effect demonstrable by platelet-function assays. MSK flags antiplatelet therapy as the main interaction concern.

Gingerols inhibit thromboxane synthesis and platelet aggregation; case reports describe elevated INR and epistaxis with warfarin co-use. MSK advises ginger-supplement discontinuation 2 weeks before surgery.

sources.name: NIH ODS Fact Sheet — Omega-3 Fatty Acids (Health Professional)

notes: High-dose (>3 g/day) EPA/DHA reduces platelet aggregation via eicosanoid shift, modestly prolonging bleeding time. NIH ODS lists anticoagulants among key drug-interaction categories to monitor.

sources.name: NIH ODS Fact Sheet — Omega-3 Fatty Acids (Health Professional)

notes: NIH ODS notes an additive antiplatelet effect with aspirin, with small but detectable prolongation of bleeding time. Clinically relevant bleeding risk is low at dietary doses but non-trivial with pharmacologic dosing.

Case reports describe excessive intraoperative bleeding in patients on saw palmetto; mechanism is likely COX-inhibition plus mild platelet effect. MSK and LiverTox both flag bleeding risk with anticoagulants.

Chamomile contains coumarin derivatives (umbelliferone, herniarin) with weak anticoagulant activity. Case reports describe INR destabilisation with concurrent warfarin; MSK recommends caution and INR monitoring.
Fenugreek seeds contain dicoumarol-like compounds; case reports document INR elevation when added to warfarin. MSK advises holding fenugreek before procedures and monitoring INR.

sources.name: MSK About Herbs — St. John's Wort

notes: SJW is a potent P-glycoprotein inducer. A randomised controlled trial showed a 25% drop in digoxin AUC after 10 days of co-administration, risking loss of rate control in atrial fibrillation and heart-failure decompensation.
Fexofenadine is a P-gp substrate with minimal CYP metabolism; chronic SJW reduces fexofenadine exposure via intestinal P-gp induction, blunting antihistaminic efficacy.
Grapefruit juice inhibits intestinal OATP1A2/P-gp influx, reducing fexofenadine absorption in crossover PK studies and lowering antihistamine effect — the opposite sign of the felodipine interaction.
Curcumin is a P-gp inhibitor in vitro and can increase intracellular accumulation of P-gp substrates such as paclitaxel. MSK advises avoiding curcumin supplements during taxane chemotherapy.

Silybin inhibits P-gp efflux in in-vitro and animal models, increasing accumulation of vinca-alkaloid substrates. MSK lists chemotherapy agents as an interaction category; clinical significance in humans is uncertain but plausible.

A randomised placebo-controlled trial showed Asian ginseng reduced warfarin peak INR and AUC, a pharmacodynamic antagonism likely mediated by ginsenoside Rd and induced vitamin-K-dependent clotting-factor synthesis.

Glycyrrhizin inhibits 11β-HSD2, producing pseudo-hyperaldosteronism with hypokalaemia, sodium retention, and hypertension. Added to loop diuretics this precipitates severe hypokalaemia and arrhythmia; case reports include torsades de pointes.

Licorice-induced sodium retention and hypertension directly antagonise the blood-pressure-lowering effect of ACE inhibitors; MSK explicitly warns against licorice use in patients with hypertension or on antihypertensives.
Glycyrrhizin-driven cortisol exposure can raise fasting glucose and blunt oral hypoglycaemic effect. MSK advises caution when licorice is taken alongside diabetes medication.

Yohimbine is a central α2-adrenergic antagonist — the exact pharmacologic opposite of clonidine. Co-administration reverses antihypertensive effect and has precipitated hypertensive crisis in published case reports; MSK lists yohimbe as contraindicated in hypertension.
